app.title: Traumatrees
learn.title: Understanding intergenerational trauma
learn.lede: A short, plain introduction to the ideas behind Traumatrees: what intergenerational trauma is, and how mapping a family can make its patterns visible.
learn.whatGlance: What happened before you can still move through you.
learn.whatBody1: Intergenerational trauma, also called generational or inherited trauma, describes how the effects of deeply difficult experiences can carry from one generation to the next. War, loss, addiction, abuse, displacement, poverty: events like these shape not only the people who live through them, but often the ways their children and grandchildren learn to talk, trust, and cope.
learn.whatBody2: Families are systems. Family systems thinking, developed by Murray Bowen and others, looks at a family as an emotional unit in which roles, rules, and silences are passed along, mostly without anyone deciding to pass them. A grandmother's unspoken grief can become a mother's vigilance, and a child's sense that some subjects are simply not raised.
learn.whatBody3: Researchers such as Rachel Yehuda have studied how the effects of severe stress may echo in later generations, including possible biological pathways. The science is still young and careful, and none of it means a family's past is its destiny. Understanding context is not a diagnosis; it is a starting point for reflection.
learn.mapTitle: How family tree mapping helps reveal generational patterns
learn.mapGlance: Patterns hide in stories until you lay them side by side.
learn.mapBody1: Most family stories live in fragments: a remark at a birthday, a name nobody mentions, a year that is skipped over. Family history mapping brings those fragments into one view. When you place people, relationships, and significant events on a family tree across generations, family trauma patterns that were invisible in conversation can become visible on paper.
learn.mapBody2: Repetition is often the first thing people see: losses that cluster around the same age, addiction that returns in every generation, moves and migrations that echo each other, silences that repeat. Seeing a pattern does not explain it, and it does not assign blame. It gives you something concrete to reflect on, to write about, or to bring to a professional if you choose to.
learn.mapBody3: Traumatrees was built for exactly this kind of quiet looking: a private place to map what happened, notice what repeats, and decide for yourself what it means and what you want to do with it.
learn.ref1Label: APA Dictionary of Psychology: intergenerational trauma
learn.ref1Desc: A short, careful definition from the American Psychological Association.
learn.ref2Label: Yehuda and Lehrner (2018), Intergenerational transmission of trauma effects
learn.ref2Desc: An open-access research overview of how trauma effects may carry across generations.
learn.ref3Label: The Bowen Center: introduction to family systems theory
learn.ref3Desc: The eight concepts of Bowen family systems theory, explained plainly.
learn.ref4Label: The National Child Traumatic Stress Network
learn.closing: If you want to try this for yourself, you can create a tree whenever you are ready. There is no rush.
tour.title: A short tour of Traumatrees
tour.lede: Three views of the same fictional family: the canvas, the timeline, and the patterns. This is what working in Traumatrees looks like.
tour.note: Everything on this page comes from a fictional demo family, created to show the tool. Inside your own account you can create the same demo tree to explore safely.
tour.canvasBody: Every person is a card. Relationships are drawn as lines: solid for biological, dashed for step and adoptive ties, a separate colour for partners. Small shapes on a card mark what happened to that person: circles for trauma, squares for life events, triangles for classifications.
tour.timelineBody: The timeline lays every life out as a bar, generation by generation. Events sit on the years they happened, so you can see what overlapped: who was a child when, what came after what, and where lives ran in parallel.
tour.patternsBody: Patterns link related events across people and generations: the same kind of loss, the same silence, the same age. Each pattern has its own colour and its own card, so you can step back and look at what repeats.
tour.shotPatternsAlt: Two pattern cards linking related events across people and generations
tour.closing: When you are ready, you can map your own family. There is no rush.

prompt.journal.1: Who in your family tree do you most identify with, and why?
prompt.journal.2: What event had the most ripple effects across generations?

prompt.journal.8: If you could ask one ancestor a question, who and what would it be?
prompt.journal.9: What did your family teach you about handling difficult emotions?
prompt.journal.10: Where do you see yourself in this tree?

landing.heroTagline: A quiet place to map the patterns you carry.
landing.tagline: Map intergenerational trauma onto visual family trees.
landing.about: Traumatrees is a personal reflection tool for exploring patterns of trauma across generations. Build your family tree, record events, and visualize how experiences ripple through time.
landing.feature1: Build visual family trees with relationships across generations
landing.feature2: Record trauma events, life events, and classifications
landing.feature3: Discover recurring patterns that ripple through time
landing.privacy: Everything you enter is encrypted on your device before it ever reaches our server. We use zero-knowledge encryption, meaning we cannot read your data, even if we wanted to. Your encryption key is the only key that can unlock your information. We don't store it, and we can't recover it. If you lose your encryption key, your data is gone forever. This is by design: your story belongs to no one but you.
landing.heroIntro: Traumatrees helps you map intergenerational trauma on a visual family tree, one quiet step at a time.
landing.whatBody: A personal reflection tool, not therapy and not crisis support. You map your family across generations, mark the events and patterns you notice, and write at your own pace. Nothing here is urgent.
landing.step1Body: Add the people who shaped you, and the generations before them. Draw the relationships as they really were.
landing.step2Body: Note trauma events, life events, and classifications on a person. Small shapes keep each node readable: circles for trauma, squares for life events, triangles for classifications.
landing.step3Body: Link related events across people and generations, then step back and reflect on what repeats, and where it might end with you.
landing.privacyGlance: We can never read your data.
landing.shotTreeAlt: A family tree on the Traumatrees canvas, with badges for trauma, life events, and classifications
landing.shotTimelineAlt: A timeline of the generations, with life bars and event markers
landing.shotTimelineCaption: The timeline view: every life as a bar, with events marked along the way.
landing.whoBody: Anyone trying to understand the patterns they carry: what was passed down, what was never spoken about, and what they want to set down. You do not need a diagnosis, a complete family history, or any answers yet.
landing.learnTeaser: Difficult experiences can echo from one generation to the next, in how a family talks, copes, and connects. Mapping your family makes those echoes easier to see. We wrote a short introduction to the ideas behind Traumatrees.
landing.founderBody1: Traumatrees started with a practical problem: my own family turned out to be too complex for the usual genealogy tools. They assume one shape of family, and real families rarely cooperate. Step-parents, second marriages, half-siblings, two mothers raising a child: I kept running into things the software simply could not say.
landing.founderBody2: I also wanted to note what had happened in those lives, not just who was related to whom, and that is where every tool stopped feeling safe. I have built software for long enough to know how data is treated once it sits on someone else's server. Notes about a family's hardest moments should not live there in readable form. So Traumatrees encrypts everything on your device, and nothing readable ever reaches us.
landing.founderBody3: It is a reflection tool, not therapy, and it does not try to be more than that. If it helps you see your own family more clearly, it is doing what it was built for.
landing.faqA1: No. Traumatrees is a tool for personal reflection. It is not therapy, counselling, or crisis support. If you are in crisis, please reach out to a professional or a local helpline.
landing.faqQ2: Can you read what I write?
landing.faqA2: No. Your entries are encrypted on your device before they reach our server, using Argon2id and AES-256-GCM. We store only unreadable ciphertext and have no way to decrypt it.
landing.faqQ3: What if I lose my encryption key?
landing.faqA3: Then your data is gone, and no one, including us, can recover it. That is the cost of encryption only you can open. Keep your encryption key somewhere safe, and consider setting a hint.
landing.faqQ4: Do I have to map everything at once?
landing.faqA4: No. You set the pace. Start with one person, leave the rest, and come back whenever you want.
landing.faqA5: Hardship that echoes from one generation to the next. Events in the lives of parents and grandparents can shape how a family talks, copes, and connects, long after the events themselves. Mapping them can make those echoes visible.
landing.faqA6: Only you. Everything is encrypted on your device before it reaches our server, and your encryption key never leaves your device. There is no sharing feature and no way for us to look inside.
landing.faqA7: Yes. You can delete individual entries, whole trees, or your entire account in settings. Deleting your account permanently removes all associated data from our systems.
landing.finalCtaBody: There is no rush. When you want to begin, your account takes a minute to create.
privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.collect.heading: What we collect
privacy.collect.body: When you create an account, we store your email address and a hashed version of your password for authentication purposes. We also store an encryption salt used to derive your personal encryption key. When you use the app, we store your tree data (persons, relationships, events) as encrypted blobs that we cannot read. We record login timestamps to monitor service health and detect abuse.
privacy.collect.glance: We store your email, a scrambled version of your password, and your tree data in encrypted form that we cannot read.
privacy.cannotAccess.heading: What we cannot access
privacy.cannotAccess.body: All tree content (names, birth years, relationship details, trauma event descriptions, and any other information you enter) is encrypted on your device before it reaches our server. We store this data as opaque ciphertext. We have no way to decrypt, read, or analyse your content. Even under a legal order, we could not produce your plaintext data because we do not have your encryption key.
privacy.cannotAccess.glance: We can never read your names, dates, relationships, or events. Everything is encrypted before it leaves your device. Even if ordered by a court, we could not hand over your data.
privacy.encryption.heading: How encryption works
privacy.encryption.body: When you register, you choose an encryption key separate from your login password. Your encryption key, combined with a random salt, derives a 256-bit AES-GCM key using Argon2id (a memory-hard key derivation function). That derived key exists only in your browser's memory while you are logged in. Every piece of data is encrypted with a fresh random initialisation vector before being sent to the server. When you close the tab or log out, the key is discarded. We never store, transmit, or log your encryption key or the key it derives.
privacy.encryption.glance: Your encryption key derives a working key that lives only in your browser while you are logged in. When you close the tab, the key is gone. We never see it.
privacy.hosting.heading: Hosting and infrastructure
privacy.hosting.body: The application runs on Google Cloud Platform (Cloud Run and Cloud SQL) in the EU. Email verification messages are sent via SMTP. We do not use any third-party analytics, tracking scripts, advertising networks, or content delivery networks that process your data.
privacy.hosting.glance: Our servers run in the EU on Google Cloud. We use no analytics, no trackers, and no advertising networks.
privacy.cookies.heading: Cookies and local storage
privacy.cookies.body: We do not use cookies. We store two JSON Web Tokens (authentication tokens) in your browser's local storage to keep you logged in. These tokens contain your user ID and expiration time; no personal content. We do not use any third-party scripts that set cookies or track your behaviour.
privacy.cookies.glance: No cookies. We only store a login token in your browser to keep you logged in. No tracking of any kind.
privacy.retention.heading: Data retention
privacy.retention.body: Your data is stored for as long as your account exists. If you delete your account, all associated data (encrypted blobs, login records, and account information) is permanently removed from our systems.
privacy.retention.glance: Your data exists as long as your account does. Delete your account and everything is permanently removed.
privacy.rights.heading: Your rights
privacy.rights.body: Under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), you have the right to access your personal data, rectify inaccurate data, request erasure of your data, restrict or object to processing, and receive your data in a portable format. You also have the right to lodge a complaint with your local data protection authority. Note that for encrypted content, you already have full access through the app; we cannot provide a plaintext copy because we do not have one.
privacy.rights.glance: Under GDPR, you can access, correct, or delete your data at any time. Since everything is encrypted, you already have full access through the app.
privacy.contact.heading: Contact
privacy.contact.body: For privacy-related questions or to exercise your rights, contact us at:
privacy.contact.glance: Questions about privacy? Reach out to us at:
safety.onboarding.whatThisIsBody: Traumatrees is a personal reflection tool. It is not therapy and not crisis support.
safety.onboarding.whatThisMayBringUpBody: Exploring family patterns can be emotionally charged. You set the pace. Pause or stop whenever you want.
safety.onboarding.tryDemoBody: Not sure where to start? Create a demo tree with fictional data to explore all features first.
safety.onboarding.whatWeCannotSeeBody: Your data is encrypted before it leaves your device. The server sees no content.

welcome.title: Welcome to Traumatrees
welcome.body: This is an early version of the app. Start by creating your first tree, and if anything feels off or you have ideas, we'd genuinely love to hear from you.
privacy.diagram.technicalBody: Key derivation: Argon2id (memory-hard, 64 MB, 3 iterations). Encryption: AES-256-GCM with fresh random IV per operation. Implementation: Web Crypto API (browser-native, no external libraries).

security.title: How your login and encryption key protect your data
security.subtitle: Two steps, two different jobs. Your login opens your account; your encryption key locks your private entries on your own device.
security.step1.title: Login and password, to reach your account
security.step1.note: Your email and password are used only to sign in and confirm account access.
security.step1.card1.title: You log in
security.step1.card1.body: Your email and password identify your account.
security.step1.card2.title: Access is checked
security.step1.card2.body: The server confirms that you may sign in.
security.step1.card3.title: Your account opens
security.step1.card3.body: You can use the app once you are logged in.
security.step1.card4.title: Nothing is decrypted
security.step1.card4.body: Your login password does not unlock your private entries.
security.step2.title: Encryption key, to lock your data on your device
security.step2.note: Your encryption key never leaves your device. It is used before any entry is sent.
security.step2.card1.title: You set an encryption key
security.step2.card1.body: You remember it. The server never learns it.
security.step2.card2.title: A key is derived
security.step2.card2.body: Your device turns the encryption key into a strong key.
security.step2.card3.title: Your entries are encrypted
security.step2.card3.body: Each entry is encrypted on your device before upload.
security.step2.card4.title: Only ciphertext is sent
security.step2.card4.body: Unreadable encrypted data travels to the server.
security.step2.card5.title: We store ciphertext
security.step2.card5.body: We keep encrypted blobs we have no way to open.
security.answer.heading: No.
security.answer.body: Your entries are encrypted on your device before they reach our server, using Argon2id and AES-256-GCM. We store only unreadable ciphertext and have no way to decrypt it.
security.footer: Your password opens the door. Your encryption key locks the contents.
security.access.title: When you access your data
security.access.item2: We send you your encrypted data
security.access.item3: Your device uses your encryption key (step 2) to decrypt it locally
security.why.title: Why this matters
security.why.item1: Your encryption key is the only key
security.why.item2: Your data is encrypted before it leaves your device
security.why.item4: We cannot read your data. Only you can.
security.link: See how your data is protected
